reasoning: The index is currently near recent lows around 6.55%, shaped largely by historically tight credit spreads and expectations of gradual Federal Reserve rate cuts. The over-arching linear trend from late 2025 is slightly downward. In early January, new capital deployment typically creates strong demand for high-yield credit (the 'January effect'), which structurally tightens spreads and puts further downward pressure on effective yields in the short term. The initial spike to 6.55% from the early January jobs data is thus likely to mildly retrace over the upcoming week. Consequently, for the short-term Jan 11 horizon, the probability of exceeding the 2026-01-04 baseline is evaluated at under 50%. As the forecast horizon extends to February and April, early-year seasonal technicals fade and typical macroeconomic uncertainty increases, including potential spread widenings and Federal Reserve policy adjustments. Following standard forecasting methodology over increasing horizons, the predictive probabilities are drawn steadily closer to the 0.50 level.
